content.text: EV Operating System - Hartalkar Innovations

content.caption: Hartalkar Innovations Pvt. Ltd. — Jabalpur-based EV startup building next-generation electric SUVs.

content.text: A **custom Linux-based operating system** built from scratch for electric vehicles, developed during an internship at **Hartalkar Innovations Pvt. Ltd.** — a Jabalpur-based EV startup focused on electric SUV innovation. The OS was designed to run on embedded EV hardware, with a custom kernel, stripped-down userspace, a React-based GUI via WebView, and a Node.js telemetry server for vehicle data.

content.title: Company
content.text: **Hartalkar Innovations Pvt. Ltd.** is building next-generation electric SUVs with a focus on efficiency and sustainability. Website: https://www.hartalkarinnovations.com/
The Problem

Commercial EV infotainment and control systems are either expensive licensed platforms (QNX, Android Automotive) or generic Linux distros bloated with unnecessary components. For a startup building a custom EV from the ground up, neither option is viable — the OS needs to be **lean, purpose-built, and fully owned**.

content.title: Constraints
content.text: Must boot fast on embedded hardware, run a real-time GUI for the driver, stream vehicle telemetry, and stay as lightweight as possible — no desktop environment, no unnecessary kernel modules, no bloat.
Solution Overview

A **custom Arch Linux-based OS** built using Archiso — stripped of everything non-essential, with a custom-compiled kernel configured specifically for EV hardware via Kconfig. The GUI runs as a React app rendered inside a WebView, and a Node.js server handles real-time vehicle telemetry. Unity was used for 3D vehicle simulations.
Key Components

items: **Custom Linux Kernel** — configured from scratch via Kconfig, compiled specifically for target EV hardware.
**Archiso Base** — minimal Arch Linux foundation, stripped of all non-essential packages and services.
**React GUI via WebView** — driver-facing interface built in React, rendered in-process via a WebView bridge.
**Node.js Telemetry Server** — real-time vehicle data ingestion, processing, and streaming.
**Unity 3D Simulations** — vehicle simulations for testing UI interactions and telemetry visualizations.
**v1 Prototype** — initial version built on Tiny Core Linux before pivoting to Arch for flexibility and control.

content.title: Why Arch?
content.text: Tiny Core Linux (v1) was too restrictive for the GUI and telemetry requirements. **Arch Linux** via Archiso gave full control over every package, service, and kernel module — making it the right base for a production EV OS.
Custom Linux Kernel via Kconfig

The most technically demanding part of the project — configuring and compiling a **custom Linux kernel** tailored to the EV hardware. Kconfig was used to methodically enable only the required drivers, subsystems, and features — disabling everything else to minimize boot time and memory footprint.

content.code:
# Kernel configuration workflow
cd linux-6.x.x

# Start from a minimal base config
make allnoconfig

# Open menuconfig to selectively enable required modules
make menuconfig
# → Enable: required CPU arch, GPIO, I2C, CAN bus, display drivers
# → Disable: sound, bluetooth, wifi (not needed on target hardware)
# → Tune: preemption model, timer frequency, memory management

# Compile the kernel
make -j$(nproc)

# Install modules
make modules_install

# Copy kernel image
cp arch/x86/boot/bzImage /boot/vmlinuz-ev

content.text: Key Kconfig Decisions

items: **CONFIG_PREEMPT** - Enabled for low-latency response in the driver UI.
**CONFIG_HZ=1000** - High timer frequency for responsive telemetry polling.
**CAN Bus support** - Enabled for vehicle ECU communication over CAN.
**Framebuffer drivers** - Minimal display support for the WebView GUI.
**Stripped modules** - Bluetooth, WiFi, sound, and unused filesystems all disabled.

content.title: Research Intensive
content.text: Kconfig has thousands of options. Getting a minimal kernel that still boots, drives the display, and communicates with vehicle hardware required extensive research into Linux driver subsystems, device trees, and hardware documentation.
OS Build with Archiso

**Archiso** is Arch Linux's official tool for building custom bootable images. It was used as the foundation to assemble the EV OS — defining exactly which packages, configs, and services go into the final image.

content.code:
ev-os/
├── airootfs/
│   ├── etc/
│   │   ├── systemd/system/     # custom systemd units
│   │   │   ├── ev-gui.service  # starts React WebView on boot
│   │   │   └── telemetry.service # starts Node.js telemetry server
│   │   └── hostname            # ev-os
│   └── usr/local/bin/
│       └── ev-launcher         # boot entrypoint
├── packages.x86_64             # minimal package list
├── profiledef.sh               # image config
└── build.sh                    # build script

# Build the bootable ISO
mkarchiso -v -w /tmp/archiso-work -o /out ./ev-os/

# Flash to hardware
dd if=/out/ev-os.iso of=/dev/sdX bs=4M status=progress

content.text: Filesystem Structure

content.code:
/
├── boot/
│   └── vmlinuz-ev          ← custom compiled kernel
├── etc/
│   └── systemd/system/
│       ├── ev-gui.service   ← React WebView autostart
│       └── telemetry.service← Node.js server autostart
├── usr/
│   ├── bin/                 ← stripped to essentials only
│   └── local/
│       ├── ev-gui/          ← React app bundle
│       └── telemetry/       ← Node.js telemetry server
├── var/
│   └── log/telemetry/       ← vehicle data logs
└── tmp/

content.title: v1 - Tiny Core Linux
content.text: The first prototype was built on **Tiny Core Linux** — a ~16MB minimal Linux distro. It validated the WebView + React GUI concept but lacked the flexibility needed for kernel customization and telemetry. The project pivoted to Arch in v2.
Driver GUI — React + WebView

The driver-facing interface is a **React application** rendered inside a native WebView — giving the performance of a native app with the flexibility of web technologies. The GUI displays real-time telemetry, vehicle status, and controls — all fed by the local Node.js telemetry server.

items: **React frontend** — component-based UI for speed, battery, temperature, and vehicle status.
**WebView bridge** — React app rendered natively, no browser chrome, no URL bar.
**Local API** — GUI fetches data from `localhost` Node.js telemetry server via REST/WebSocket.
**Auto-start on boot** — `ev-gui.service` systemd unit launches WebView immediately after kernel init.

content.title: Why WebView?
content.text: A full browser or desktop environment (X11/Wayland + Electron) would add hundreds of MBs to the image. **WebView** renders the React app directly with a minimal native wrapper — fast boot, small footprint, same dev experience.
Node.js Telemetry Server

A **Node.js server** runs on the OS to ingest, process, and stream real-time vehicle telemetry data — speed, battery state, temperature, motor RPM, and CAN bus events. The GUI subscribes to this server via WebSocket for live updates.

content.title: CAN Bus
content.text: Vehicle ECUs communicate over **CAN (Controller Area Network)** — the standard automotive protocol. The telemetry server reads raw CAN frames, decodes them into readable metrics, and streams them to the GUI in real time.
Unity 3D Simulations

**Unity** was used to build 3D vehicle simulations for testing and demonstrating the OS — visualizing telemetry data spatially, simulating driving scenarios, and validating GUI interactions before hardware was available.

items: **Vehicle dashboard simulation** — 3D cockpit view driven by live telemetry data.
**Battery and motor state visualization** — real-time 3D representation of EV drivetrain.
**Scenario testing** — simulate edge cases (low battery, overheat alerts) without physical hardware.

content.text: Tech Stack

items: **Arch Linux + Archiso** - Custom bootable OS image builder.
**Linux Kernel (custom)** - Compiled from source via Kconfig for target EV hardware.
**Tiny Core Linux** - v1 prototype base (later replaced by Arch).
**React** - Driver-facing GUI.
**WebView** - Native wrapper to render React app without a full browser.
**Node.js** - Real-time vehicle telemetry server over WebSocket + CAN bus.
**Unity** - 3D vehicle simulations and dashboard prototyping.
**systemd** - Service management for auto-starting GUI and telemetry on boot.
**C / Assembly** - Low-level hardware interfacing and kernel module work.

content.text: IP & Confidentiality Note

content.title: Proprietary Work
content.text: The GUI source code and OS image are proprietary to **Hartalkar Innovations Pvt. Ltd.** and cannot be publicly shared. This documentation covers the technical architecture and decisions made during the internship. The kernel configuration, build scripts, and simulation work are described at a high level only.
